observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor request the SB to pass a resolution authorizing the transfer of Barangay Health Centers to the various recipient barangays and, and then prepare Deeds of Donation for the actual transfer and acceptance by the latter to substantiate the transfer of ownership of the said properties.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct his administrative officer to:
promptly furnish documents to the MA, Property and Supply Officer and ME, in cases of receipt of PPE, to ensure timely inspection and recognition of the same in the financial statements and to their respective records;
inform the DPWH on the PPE that are not functional; and
furnish the SB of the results of the inspection of the ME to facilitate the preparation of SB Resolution accepting the functional PPE transferred by the DPWH.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct both the Supply Officer and the MA to:
maintain updated inventory records based on the receipt, acceptance, and issuance of inventory items as prescribed by the Manual;
strictly follow the procedures set forth in Manual; and
conduct semestral (or oftener depending on the discretion of management) physical count of inventory items to be reported in the RPCI which should be submitted to the Audit Team’s office.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider allocating a room or space for stockroom of inventory items purchased.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
The ME to strictly adhere to the provisions and guidelines in the preparation of the ABC and perform a detailed review of the POW to ensure that the basis and ceiling of bid prices are acceptable/reasonable and advantageous to the government, thus, avoiding excessive contract cost;
The BAC and TWG to ensure in their evaluation the correct computation of detailed estimates of the contractors considering those items not subject to OCM and Profit mark-ups; and
The MA, in coordination with the ME, to require the concerned contractors to refund the amount of ₱144,247.63 broken down as follows:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the ME to:
strictly require all contractors to ensure their entire workforce is wearing the required safety gear or personal protective equipment at all times within the construction project site for the protection and welfare of both the construction workers and the general public;
closely monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP and Standards by the contractors at the project sites by maintaining detailed records of contractor’s non-compliance per site visitation, which can be referenced during future payment processes; and
formulate an internal policy that clearly defines the appropriate sanctions or penalties to be applied to contractors found in violation of the CSHP to ensure accountability and adherence to safety protocols.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
Human Resource Development Officer to strictly monitor payroll deductions by maintaining employee ledgers as the basis for approval of loan applications;
Human Resource Development Officer and the Municipal Treasurer to refrain from signing certifications for loan applications should an employee’s net take-home pay fall below the mandatory amount of P5,000.00, exclusive of specific-purpose allowances; and
Human Resource Development Officer to conduct an extensive information campaign for all employees to ensure a comprehensive understanding of loan-related matters, empower employees with essential knowledge, and promote financial literacy across the organization.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned department heads to comply with the provisions of Section 16 of the IRR of RA No. 9433 and DBM-DOH Joint Circular No. 1 s 2016, such as: a) preparing a list of positions in its plantilla with the corresponding job description or actual duties that describes the basis for justifying such positions for those who are exposed to occupational risks and assigned to places as stated in Section 16; and b) establishing a procedure for identifying personnel who shall be exposed to hazardous and occupational risks as stated in the Section, including the duration of exposure.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MA that claims for HP of Social Workers, Health Workers, be supported with proof of actual service rendered during the period being claimed and that the same be computed in accordance with Section 16 of the IRR of RA No. 9433 for Social Workers and DBM-DOH JC No. 1 s 2016 for Health Workers and Meat Inspectors.
Further, we also recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the liable persons to submit the lacking documents such as the DTR and Accomplishment Reports for CY2023 to the audit team for further evaluation of the transactions. Moreover, we recommend refunding the overpayment amounting to ₱68,639.40.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor or his authorized representative inform the sellers that they will bear the estate tax on their acquired property amounting to ₱57,928.50 and thereafter, direct the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the transfer of the property in the name of the Municipality.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concern department heads to ensure that in the succeeding contracts on the acquisition of parcels of land, include in the agreement that, the seller will shoulder the payment of transfer taxes, estate tax and/or CGT and DST.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the HRMO to:
revisit and study the organizational structure of the municipality, particularly the staffing pattern of all its offices, and consider abolishing items not considered vital, specifically those that have been left vacant for a long period; and
fill up all vacant positions that are still considered vital by hiring new employees or expediting the promotion of deserving job-order employees.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDC to:
ensure that that enumerated list of PPAs embodied in the LDIP of the Municipality is of priority to be implemented to guarantee that PPAs are beneficial to the localities and to ensure that implemented projects have the corresponding budget;
prepare a well-defined AIP in accordance with the harmonization and synchronization of local planning, investment programming, revenue administration budgeting, and expenditure management prescribed in DILG-NEDA-DBM-DOF JMC No. 1 series of 2007 dated March 8, 2007 that will make way for the achievement of long-term goals of the Municipality; and
update the LDIP in case there are changes in the PPAs to be implemented, which will serve as the basis for preparing and implementing the AIP for the budget year.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE) enjoin the Barangay Officials to inform the community about applicable laws and ordinances, and the corresponding fines and penalties for non-compliance. While ignorance of the law excuses no one, prevention by information is the sounder approach than imposition of penalties.  As provided in RA No. 7160, the Barangay is the basic political unit–it serves as the primary planning and implementing unit of government policies, plans, programs, projects, and activities in the community.  The barangay is a principal link of the people/constituents to the municipal/provincial and national government and vice versa.  There is none better than the Barangay Officials in the dissemination of applicable laws and ordinances, either through formal or informal setting.  However, if the laws, rules, and regulations will still not be adhered by the people, we recommend the strict compliance of the fines and penalties under the Building Code of the Philippines.

In connection to these, the Local Chief Executive is also recommended to encourage the Municipal and Barangay Officials alike to coordinate with the Municipal Building Official, information about structures being built within their jurisdiction, primarily, in order to assure the safety and security of the community from possible effect of defective construction.

On the other hand, we have additionally recommended that the Municipal Assessor conduct frequent physical surveys to verify and determine whether all real properties within the province are properly listed in the assessment rolls and to exercise the functions of appraisal and assessment primarily for taxation purposes of all real properties in the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to enjoin:
the Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of infrastructure assets;
the Municipal Engineer in providing the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects; and
the Property Officer – Designate and the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the required records and reports under COA Circular No. 2015-008 for the fair presentation of the Local Road Network account in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer as to the needed supporting documents for the unadjusted reconciling items and thereafter, make the appropriate adjustments.  We have also recommended the timely preparation of monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements so that identified reconciling items be immediately adjusted in order to reflect the timely and accurate cash position of the Municipality.
Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer to trace the details on the long outstanding checks particularly as to its date and its whereabouts, and thereafter, coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to make the proper adjustment on these cancelled checks.

We have recommended that Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct:
the Municipal Treasurer to establish the Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable accounts at the beginning of the year based on the amount due and collectible for the year as supported by Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card; and
the Municipal Accountant to prepare necessary journal entries to correct the balances of Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable accounts as well as the Deferred Real Property Tax Income and Deferred Special Education Tax Income in order to fairly present said accounts.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the correcting entry reclassifying the Construction in Progress–Buildings and Other Structures to Computer Software, Information and Communication Technology Equipment and Training Expenses amounting to P1,460,000.00, P905,000.00 and P295,000.00, respectively, and provide for the depreciation of the said property, plant and equipment in order to fairly present the said accounts and provide the users of financial statements accurate information that may be used in their decision-making.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the:
Municipal Treasurer to exert more effort in the collection/liquidation of the long outstanding loan assistance to NGOs; and assist the Aringay Tobacco Growers Association in devising strategies on how to collect the loans of its delinquent member-borrowers;
Municipal Accountant to conduct further assessment and determination of objective evidence on collectability and impairment of Due from NGOs and POs and reflect the results thereof in the financial statements; and
Municipal Agriculturist to assist the Organic Agri-Services Network of Aringay in devising strategies to operationalize the farm tractor so that income could be generated that will help them pay their loan from the municipality.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant present fairly the current portion of Loans Payable-Domestic amounting to P4,263,973.82 to conform with IPSAS 1 and provide users of financial statements information helpful in assessing the short-term liquidity of the Municipality and its ability to utilize its assets in an efficient manner.  Also, we recommend that the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant provide copy of the loan amortization to the Municipal Budget Officer for reference in the budget preparation.
We further recommend that Management enact appropriation ordinance allocating corresponding budget for the payment of loan due in compliance with Section 305(a) of Republic Act No. 7160.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the:
the Municipal Budget Officer to determine the correct balances of the Current Appropriations, and Trust Liabilities; and to reconcile her records to that of the Municipal Accountant’s to ensure the proper inclusion and presentation of funds; and
the Municipal Accountant to adjust the amounts accordingly in the Financial Statements and Report on the Receipts and Utilization for DRRM in compliance with Section 5.1.11 COA Circular No. 2012-002; and to reconcile his records to that of the Budget Officer’s, to ensure the proper inclusion and presentation of funds.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer cease in holding the treasurer position of their employee association in conformity with Section 67 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual and Section 57 of Local Treasury Operations Manual in order to facilitate sound cash management of the Municipality.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Treasurer to monitor the prompt renewal of fidelity bonds of accountable officers with the Bureau of Treasury to safeguard the municipal funds from possible losses and avoid sanctions set forth by Section 127 of PD No. 1445 and Article 236 of the Revised Penal Code.

While we acknowledged the efforts of the municipal government in the implementation of SALINTUBIG projects, we have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Water and Sanitation Council to:
Prepare a reasonable work plan for the consideration of the Sangguniang Bayan in the provision of annual funding to cover the repair and rehabilitation of impaired water facilities;
Conduct training/capacity building, in coordination with the DILG, to the staff and officers of the Barangay Water Service Providers on the operation and management of the water facilities; and
Monitor and evaluate the functionality, effectiveness, and efficiency for the benefit of the intended end-users.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:
Scrutinize the sufficiency of the Contractor’s manpower on all awarded projects especially those that have overlapping implementation period with each other under same contractor and direct the contractors to address the deficiencies noted on manpower utilization;
Conduct post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and ensure that the Post-Qualification Report contains in-depth results on the verification of technical requirements; and
Determine the applicable liquidated damages on the projects that have been delayed without the corresponding requests for extensions.

Based on the foregoing observations, we have recommended that Management:
Ensure optimum implementation of the projects/programs/activities appropriated under the 20% Development Fund by a) identifying projects which is compliant with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1, b) devising strategies on the manning of personnel to facilitate full implementation of projects and programs of the municipality, and c) considering in the planning process the circumstances (such as election ban, knowledge on the projects implemented by National Government Agencies and other Local Government Units) that may hamper the immediate implementation of projects and prepare strategies to immediately address such;
Charge the improperly appropriated projects/programs in aggregate amount of P3,068,033.50 to General Fund; and
Facilitate immediate identification of developmental projects and programs using the accumulated savings of P2,561,278.65.  Thereafter, include the identified development projects and programs in the supplemental annual investment program and supplemental budget.

We have recommended that the expenditures in relation to the Capability Building Program for Teachers and Learners’ Training Expense, Alternative Learning System and special programs should not be charged under the Special Education Fund but rather under the funds of the Department of Education.  We have also recommended that all charges under SEF be confined to expenditures compliant with the guidelines set forth in Sections 272 and 100(c) of RA No. 7160, Section 7(b) of RA No. 10410 and DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. of 2017.

We have recommended that Management design control mechanism to address the cause of delay in the submission of accounts to avoid disciplinary action and disallowance of claim under examination as provided in Section 39 of PD No. 1445.  Thereafter, submit the accounts within the prescribed period to facilitate the Audit Team in the timely review and evaluation of the financial transaction documents and for the Management to perform corrective action on the deficiencies noted thereon.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials to devise a control mechanism that will secure compliance to Section 52 of Republic Act No. 11260, thus, ensuring employee productivity and satisfying their families’ basic needs.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE) enjoin the Barangay Officials to inform the community about applicable laws and ordinances, and the corresponding fines and penalties for non-compliance. While ignorance of the law excuses no one, prevention by information is the sounder approach than imposition of penalties.  As provided in RA No. 7160, the Barangay is the basic political unit–it serves as the primary planning and implementing unit of government policies, plans, programs, projects, and activities in the community.  The barangay is a principal link of the people/constituents to the municipal/provincial and national government and vice versa.  There is none better than the Barangay Officials in the dissemination of applicable laws and ordinances, either through formal or informal setting.  However, if the laws, rules, and regulations will still not be adhered by the people, we recommend the strict compliance of the fines and penalties under the Building Code of the Philippines.
In connection to these, the Local Chief Executive is also recommended to encourage the Municipal and Barangay Officials alike to coordinate with the Municipal Building Official, information about structures being built within their jurisdiction, primarily, in order to assure the safety and security of the community from possible effect of defective construction.
On the other hand, we have additionally recommended that the Municipal Assessor conduct frequent physical surveys to verify and determine whether all real properties within the province are properly listed in the assessment rolls and to exercise the functions of appraisal and assessment primarily for taxation purposes of all real properties in the Municipality.

We have recommended the Municipal Treasurer the following:
Continue to demand settlement of tax liabilities from the identified taxpayers;
Adopt corresponding strategies to address the specific concerns of the taxpayers on non-payment of real property tax; and
Coordinate with the Provincial Treasury Office and avail of the administrative and legal remedies provided by law to enforce collection of the delinquent real property taxes.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Human Resources Management Officer to thoroughly study the organizational structure and staffing of the municipality in order to create positions which are essential in the delivery of public service to the constituents and fill-up vacant plantilla positions. We have also recommended that collecting functions should not be assigned to Job Order Contract workers since they are not permanent employees and not bonded.

We have recommended the Management to:
Strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the Municipality GAD Database to ensure an updated, operational and reliable database, which serve the GFPS in attaining an evidence-based GAD planning and policymaking; and
Comply with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 and 2016-01 on the establishment of a gender responsive monitoring and evaluation system through the GAD M&E Team’s performance of its functions to evaluate the outcomes of the LGU GAD policies, programs, and projects.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to conduct complete inventory of all the properties of the Municipality including public infrastructures;

b. Inventory Committee upon completion of the inventory, prepare and submit the required reports such as Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE), Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System; and

c. Municipal Accountant and GSO–Designate reconcile their records with the result of the inventory and investigate the differences.
observations.managementAction: Page 58 of AAR 2018 | The Management have conducted partial inventory and  Reports on Physical Count of PPE, RRPR and IIURP were submitted. Sale through public auction of unserviceable properties was also made.
observations.reason:
Physical inventory of properties was conducted and RPCPPE and IIRUP were submitted. 

The ledgers and RPCPPE was not reconciled. 

This was reiterated in part 2 of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended to the management the following:
Conduct physical inventory of all existing properties of the municipality as required under Section 124 of NGAS Manual;
Submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the auditor’s office not later than January 31 of each year; and
Maintain property cards duly reconciled with the accounting records pursuant to Sections 119 and 120 of the same manual.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the following journal entry to properly classify the accounts mentioned above for fair presentation of the financial statements, to wit:
Machinery                                                     1,057,200.00
Agricultural & Forestry Equipment          1,095,000.00
Other Machinery Equipment                         237,000.00
Government Equity                                             2,389,200.00
We have further recommended to the Local Chief Executive to coordinate with DA-BFAR in order to request documents pertaining to the cost of the CFLC to facilitate its recording in the books of the municipality, hence, to conform with PPSAS Nos. 1 and 17.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements as of December 31, 2017 so that the necessary adjustments to reconcile the books of the agency and the bank are made and to present fairly the Cash in Bank balances in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the amount of P144,595.43 be reverted back to the 20% Development Fund and the amount be charged against the funds enumerated under DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-3 dated November 29, 2007.
We have further recommended to the Local Chief Executive to refrain from charging BAC/TWG honoraria against projects funds as provided under the above regulations and that the documentary requirements to prove the validity and legality of the transactions as enumerated be submitted for verification.

We have recommended that management strictly comply with the above-cited provisions of R.A. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations and COA Circular No. 2012-001.  For the Technical Working Group (TWG) to strictly observe and comply with the requirements expressed in Section 34.3 of the revised IRR of R.A. 9184 in the post-qualification to ensure that only bidders with the lowest calculated and responsive bids are awarded the contract.
We have further recommended that management submit the required documents and submit reasons/justifications why the Concreting of Road at Immalog, Basca was not accomplished as per intended completion date.
We have recommended and the management agreed to send demand letters to the recipients so that other proponents would be benefitted.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
to revisit the organizational structure of the municipality so as to create positions which are essential in the delivery of public service to the constituents and to lessen the number of JOCs;
JOCs should not be involved in the handling of funds of the municipality since they are not permanent employees and they are not bonded; and
submit certification that the activities/services cannot be provided by regular and permanent personnel to prove the validity and legality of the transactions.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to comply with the regulations cited above so as not to affect the benefits accruing to the officials and employees of the municipality.  In doing so, penalties, if any, that may be imposed on the employer, will be avoided.  We have further recommended that the Municipal Accountant look into the reasons why there were over-remittances.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to prioritize the establishment of GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation as required under the above regulation.

We have recommended and the management agreed:
to require the barangay or cluster of barangays to establish a Material Recovery Facility;
mandatory segregation of solid waste be implemented; and
to prioritize the implementation of an effective and efficient waste disposal facility as required under the above-mentioned regulation to protect the environment and the constituents.

We have recommended that the conduct of benchmarking or lakbay aral be in accordance with the policies and guidelines as provided in Section 2 of P.D. 1445, DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 02, and EO 298 and to comply with the documentary requirement.   Further, prudence should be exercise at all times in the use of public funds.

We have recommended and the management agreed to coordinate with DA-BFAR for the defects noted and to comply with the Memorandum of Agreement mentioned above so that the CFLC will be utilized in accordance with the purpose for which it was established.
We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to implement the priority programs and projects which remained unimplemented inorder to uplift the lives of its constituents.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the LCPC to implement the programs, projects and activities funded out of the one per cent of IRA so as not to hamper the development and protection benefits that should have been enjoyed by the children as mandated under the above regulations.

We have recommended and the management agreed to:
notify the contractors about the defects and require them to do corrective actions to prolong the useful life of these infrastructure projects; and
require the Project Monitoring Team to perform regular and thorough monitoring and supervision of projects to ensure that only those satisfactorily completed and in accordance with the specifications are paid.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Treasurer to supervise and monitor market collections.  For an effective operation of the market, income should be enough to cover its maintenance.

We have recommended to management to see to it that all the required supporting documents be attached to the disbursement vouchers before payment be made to avoid suspension or disallowance in the future.

We have recommended to the Local Chief Executive to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the General Service Office to reconcile their records on Property, Plant and Equipment as required under the above mentioned regulation in order to present fairly the financial statements of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: Continue inventory on the 2nd quarter of 2018.
observations.reason: The General Service Officer (GSO) took a leave of absence during the last 2 weeks of the year, hence, no inventory report was made.
